subject: ap-precalculus
title: Rational & Piecewise Functions: Asymptotes, Inverses & Transformations
stimulus: The function $f$ is defined by $f(x) = \frac{3x + 6}{x - 2}$ for $x \neq 2$. The function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = \ln(x - 1) + 4$ for $x > 1$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Determine the equations of any vertical and horizontal asymptotes of $f$. Explain how each asymptote relates to the structure of the function.

(ii) Determine whether $f$ has an inverse function. If it does, find $f^{-1}(x)$. If it does not, explain why.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct asymptotes — vertical asymptote $x = 2$ (denominator equals zero) and horizontal asymptote $y = 3$ (ratio of leading coefficients as $x \to \pm\infty$, since $\frac{3x+6}{x-2} \to 3$); both must be present with correct equations

1 pt: Correct inverse — $f$ has an inverse (it is one-to-one: each horizontal line $y = k \neq 3$ intersects the graph at most once); $f^{-1}(x) = \frac{2x + 6}{x - 3}$ (derived by solving $y = \frac{3x+6}{x-2}$ for $x$: $y(x-2) = 3x+6 \Rightarrow xy - 2y = 3x + 6 \Rightarrow x(y-3) = 2y+6 \Rightarrow x = \frac{2y+6}{y-3}$)
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), the explanation of vertical asymptote should reference 'denominator equals zero at $x = 2$.' The horizontal asymptote explanation should reference degree comparison or limit behavior. For part (ii), the domain of $f^{-1}$ is $x \neq 3$. Accept any equivalent form of $f^{-1}(x)$. Note $f$ is actually a shifted hyperbola and is strictly decreasing on each piece, so it is one-to-one on its domain $x \neq 2$.

parts.prompt:
(i) Find the value of $g^{-1}(6)$, where $g^{-1}$ is the inverse of $g$. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Find all values of $x$ for which $(f \circ g)(x) = 4$, or indicate there are no such values. Express your answer as a decimal approximation.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct value $g^{-1}(6) = e^2 + 1 = 8.389$ (from $\ln(x-1) + 4 = 6 \Rightarrow \ln(x-1) = 2 \Rightarrow x - 1 = e^2 \Rightarrow x = e^2 + 1$; accept exact $e^2 + 1$ or decimal approximation $8.389$)

1 pt: Correct solution — $f(g(x)) = 4$: first solve $f(u) = 4$ to get $\frac{3u+6}{u-2} = 4 \Rightarrow 3u+6 = 4u-8 \Rightarrow u = 14$; then $g(x) = 14 \Rightarrow \ln(x-1) + 4 = 14 \Rightarrow \ln(x-1) = 10 \Rightarrow x = e^{10} + 1 \approx 22027.466$
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), accept either exact or decimal form. For part (ii), the two-step approach (solve $f(u)=4$ then $g(x)=u$) is the expected method. Decimal approximation $x \approx 22027.466$ must be correct to three decimal places. Common error: solving $g(f(x)) = 4$ instead of $f(g(x)) = 4$.

parts.prompt:
The function $g(x) = \ln(x - 1) + 4$ is a transformation of $y = \ln(x)$.

(i) Describe the transformations applied to $y = \ln(x)$ to obtain $g(x)$.

(ii) Determine the end behavior of $g$ as $x$ increases without bound. Express your answer using the notation of a limit.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Correct transformations — horizontal shift right by $1$ unit (replacing $x$ with $x - 1$) and vertical shift up by $4$ units (adding $4$); both transformations must be named correctly with directions
1 pt: Correct limit statement $\lim_{x \to \infty} g(x) = \infty$, with all four components: 'lim', '$x \to \infty$', function $g$, and $\infty$
parts.scoring_notes: For part (i), accept 'shift right 1, up 4' or equivalent. Do not accept 'shift left 1' or 'shift down 4.' For part (ii), the end behavior of $\ln$ functions grows without bound (slowly), so the limit is $\infty$. Also accept: $g(x) \to \infty$ as $x \to \infty$. If student writes $\lim_{x \to \infty} g(x) = \ln(\infty) + 4$, this is not acceptable notation.
